meta.title: 23: Hyukoh and the Duality of Youth
meta.author: Mark Laurence G. Ting

body: What does it mean to be young at the exact moment you realize it will not last?

That question sits at the center of 23, the first full-length album by South Korean indie rock band Hyukoh (혁오). Released on April 24, 2017, the record is the third installment in the band's age-named discography, in which each release is titled after the members' age at the time, following EPs 20 (2014) and 22 (2015). By any measure, it is their most ambitious and fully realized work. While their earlier EPs hinted at themes of youthful anxiety, 23 explores them more fully and with greater emotional depth, examining what Oh Hyuk describes as the duality of youth, both its radiant optimism and the anxiety that sets in when you realize it is finite.

Hyukoh consists of Oh Hyuk (lead vocals, rhythm guitar, songwriter), Im Dong-geon (bass), Lim Hyun-jae (lead guitar), and Lee In-woo (drums). The band's name is taken from Oh Hyuk's own name rearranged, reflecting how central his identity is to the group's artistic direction. This focus on Oh Hyuk carries into 23's sonic identity, which is shaped by his husky, melancholic voice, often closer to a murmur than a proclamation.

23 is a 12-track full-length album featuring double title tracks ("Leather Jacket" and "TOMBOY"). It is trilingual, incorporating Korean, English, and Mandarin, and spans genres such as indie rock, surf rock, shoegaze, folk, and neo-psychedelia. This range reflects frontman Oh Hyuk's background. Born in South Korea but raised in China, he spent his first twenty years across Jilin, Shenyang, and Beijing before returning to Seoul in 2012 to pursue music. Fluent in all three languages heard on the album, his multicultural upbringing shapes its sound, blending diverse influences into a cohesive style that feels personal rather than assembled from separate genres.

Thematically, the album traces an emotional arc from the brittle bravado of early youth to grief, introspection, and, ultimately, cautious hope. It does not move through these states in a perfectly linear fashion, it lurches, doubles back, and surprises; and that unpredictability gives the album a sense of authenticity.

sections.heading: Burning Youth
body: The album opens with "Burning Youth," a short, two-minute track that wastes no time establishing tone. Its very first line, "How will you survive the world full of anguish," reads like a thesis statement for the songs that follow. Paradoxically, these lyrics are delivered over a bright, marching drum pattern and delay-soaked guitar picking that sounds almost celebratory. The tension between its resigned lyrical sentiment and energetic arrangement is intentional and effective. It sets the tone of the entire album perfectly: troubled on the inside, propulsive on the outside.

sections.heading: Tokyo Inn
body: "Tokyo Inn" continues that momentum. Driven by a jangly electric guitar with a surf-rock shimmer, the song feels like the inside of a moving taxi at night, restless, untethered, going somewhere but not quite sure where. The song's hook, the wordless, lilting "woo-woo" refrain is a dangerous earworm, and it anchors an otherwise melancholy meditation on aimless youth. What makes the track memorable is the way it pivots between its driving chorus energy and quieter moments where Oh Hyuk's falsetto floats delicately in the space, giving the song a push-and-pull tension that feels true to the emotional state it describes.

sections.heading: Leather Jacket
body: "Leather Jacket," one of the album's double title tracks, picks up the pace with a sharp, fast-paced rhythm and a bass line that gets increasingly heavy as the track progresses. Thematically, the song is a declaration of non-conformity, a refusal to be shaped by societal molds and expectations. Even the title itself evokes a leather jacket as a symbol of rebellion and individuality, reinforcing the song's defiant stance. The guitar has a noticeable twang, and the arrangement feels wiry and kinetic, mirroring the frustrated energy of someone who has been pushed to their limit and is pushing back. Like "Tokyo Inn," the song carries a defiant, memorable hook that makes it easy to return to. It is a song about exhaustion and resistance, and it sounds like both at once.

sections.heading: TOMBOY
body: Where the first three tracks bristle with energy, "TOMBOY," the album's second title track and its commercial centerpiece, strips things down into something more tender and aching. Musically, the track is built on soft guitar work and a slow, deliberate rhythm that creates space for Oh Hyuk's vocals to take center stage. Here, he sings as if he is not performing, but reflecting and confessing. The Korean lyrics, in translation, carry a bittersweet self-awareness that cuts deep: "I'm happy right now, so I'm nervous / Because the sea is always calm before the storm." It is a song about the precariousness of good moments, knowing they cannot last, loving them anyway, a feeling that captures the uncertainty of youth, where even happiness is shadowed by the awareness that it is temporary. As a comedown from the assertiveness of "Leather Jacket," it works beautifully. The duality of the album's themes, resistance and vulnerability, boldness and fear, finds its clearest expression here.

sections.heading: 2002WorldCup
body: "2002WorldCup" is arguably the album's most nostalgic track, named after the Korea-Japan World Cup that Oh Hyuk has cited as the happiest time of his childhood. The song reflects on the passage of time through small visual markers, a student with black hair, then the same person older with hair slightly dyed red, a quiet visual metaphor for how gradually people change. At the same time, these details point to a memory of young love, capturing the excitement and innocence of a relationship formed during youth and shaped as both people grow older. The production is notably livelier and more celebratory than the surrounding tracks, and the sound captures something vivid and cinematic, like a memory playing back. It also serves a structural purpose, a brief exhale before the album's darker second half.

sections.heading: Jesus Lived in a Motel Room
body: "Jesus Lived in a Motel Room" marks a tonal shift. Opening with vocal layering that gives the song an almost operatic quality before the drums kick in and bring it to a grittier, more unsettled place, the track deals with anxiety in a direct and unflinching way, not as an abstract mood but as something persistent and generational. Themes of the fraught relationship between anxiety and religion run through the lyrics, touching on parental pressure and the inherited weight of expectation. The song suggests a lingering tension rather than resolution, emphasizing the enduring nature of these anxieties.

sections.heading: Wanli 万里
body: "Wanli 万里" is the album's only track in Mandarin Chinese, and it announces itself with what sounds like approaching footsteps before a guitar line arrives that sounds less like a Western rock instrument and more like a plucked Chinese string instrument, a zither. The effect is subtle but transporting; with sparse lyrics and a title meaning "ten thousand miles" or "a great distance," the song evokes the expanse of the Great Wall, the feeling of enormous physical and emotional distance. It is one of the album's most atmospheric moments, similar to a cinematic soundtrack, where the brief, understated lines feel less like a narrative and more like an extension of the instrumental, reinforcing it rather than drawing attention away from it.

sections.heading: Die Alone
body: "Die Alone" follows with a drum-led opening that shares Wanli's sense of scale before building into one of the record's most sonically satisfying tracks. The vocal layering, including a low, repeated "hey, bum bum," gives it a ghostly, ritualistic feeling. The lyrics circle around mortality and rebirth, and the repeated refrain, rather than feeling bleak, becomes almost cathartic, a kind of release that strengthens the track's emotional impact.

sections.heading: Reserved Seat
body: "Reserved Seat" is the album's most reflective track, and also its most structurally static. The song establishes a melancholic, emo-tinged mood early, which works effectively in creating atmosphere. However, its lack of structural variation can make it feel slightly repetitive over time. Still, the layered guitars and restrained vocal delivery maintain enough emotional weight to keep it engaging.

sections.heading: Simon
body: "Simon" is among the album's most emotionally raw tracks. Written in English and dedicated to a friend of Oh Hyuk's who passed away, it carries the particular grief of speaking to someone who can no longer respond. The shoegaze-influenced production, thick with delayed, reverb-soaked guitar, creates a sound that is woozy and immersive, like a memory. The emotional power of the track comes not from any dramatic musical gesture but from a quieter observation: when you reach out to someone who is gone, there is only silence.

sections.heading: Paul
body: Where "Simon" grieves through guitar, "Paul" grieves through piano. It is quieter and slower than almost everything else on the album, and Oh Hyuk's vocal layering gives it an eerie, ghostly quality, as if multiple versions of himself are all trying to say the same thing at once. Notably, Oh Hyuk mentioned in an interview that "Paul" is actually the first song he ever wrote, its melody and lyrics evolving over years. Knowing that gives the song's themes of time and loss an additional layer.

sections.heading: Surf Boy
body: The album closes with "Surf Boy," and the tonal shift is immense and intentional. The track bursts open with bright, sun-soaked surf rock energy, vibrant, kinetic, and cheerful in a way that feels almost defiant after everything that came before it. It is summer by the beach after a long, grey and gloomy season. The lyrical message ultimately advocates for cautious optimism: hoping for the best while preparing for the worst. It is not naively hopeful, but grounded and realistic. As a closing statement, it is the right choice. The album asked, implicitly, what comes after 23? And "Surf Boy" answers: you go on, you hold on, and you continue forward with cautious hope.

As someone who is also 23 years old, I find something deeply resonant in this album, particularly in how its musical and lyrical elements capture a sense of uncertainty that feels both personal and widely relatable. Although the album was released in 2017, nearly a decade ago, the sentiments and emotional experience of being this age remain strikingly unchanged. What 23 captures, the anxiety of being between things, the simultaneous hunger for freedom and fear of what freedom costs, and the way good moments feel precarious because you know they will end, is not uniquely Korean, but reflects the condition of being this age. In listening, the album creates a quiet dialogue between the artist's perspective and the listener, leading to what can be understood as a fusion of horizons, where these emotions are recognized not as isolated, but shared.

Even without understanding Korean or Mandarin, the album communicates emotionally with remarkable clarity. Oh Hyuk's voice, husky, melancholic, yearning, and tender, is its own kind of language. It is the voice of someone who feels everything a little too much and has decided, rather than hide it, to simply write and sing it out, authentically and without restraint.

Ultimately, 23 is the kind of record that does not just hold up to repeated listening; it deepens with it. In a way, it offers an answer to the question it begins with: to be young is to feel everything intensely while knowing it will not last, to hold on to those moments anyway, even as they begin to slip away, balancing both the optimism and the quiet anxiety that come with it.

references.citation: DEW Magazine. (2017, June 13). Meet Hyukoh: The South Korean-based Indie Cool Kids - DEW Magazine. DEW Magazine. https://dewmagazine.com/meet-hyukoh-south-korean-based-indie-cool-kids/

Herman, T. (2015, August 13). Meet Hyukoh, the Indie Rock Band Topping the Korean Charts in a World of K-Pop. VICE. https://www.vice.com/en/article/hyukoh-22-comes-and-goes-south-korea-indie-rock-interview/
